course_info.title: Programming for Games
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

learning_objectives: Declare and initialize variables of common data types (int, float, string, bool)
Explain the difference between value types and reference types in C#
Use arithmetic, assignment, and string concatenation operators
Write a simple program that takes console input, processes it, and produces output
readings: Think Like a Programmer by V. Anton Spraul, Ch. 1
C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 1-2
Microsoft C# Documentation: Types and Variables (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Welcome to Programming for Games. This course teaches programming from the ground up with a focus on the patterns and problems you will encounter in game development. No prior coding experience is assumed. By the end of the semester, you will have built both a text-based RPG and a real-time 2D game.

Programming is the art of giving precise instructions to a computer. The computer does exactly what you tell it, nothing more and nothing less. This means you must be extremely specific about what you want. Every piece of data must have a type. Every operation must be spelled out. Every edge case must be handled. This precision is what makes programming challenging, but it is also what makes it powerful.

The most fundamental concept in programming is the variable: a named container in memory that holds a value. When you write 'int health = 100;', you are telling the computer to reserve space in memory, label it 'health,' and store the integer value 100 there. From that point on, you can read and modify the value by referring to its name. Variables are the building blocks of every program, and mastering them is the first step toward writing games.
key_concepts: Variable: a named location in memory that stores a value of a specific type; the name is chosen by the programmer, the type determines what operations are valid
Primitive data types: int (whole numbers, 32-bit), float (decimal numbers, ~7 digits precision), double (decimal numbers, ~15 digits precision), string (text), bool (true/false)
Variable declaration and assignment: 'type name = value;' reserves memory, labels it, and stores an initial value
Type safety: the compiler prevents operations on incompatible types (int x = "hello"; fails) to catch errors before the program runs
Arithmetic operators: + (addition), - (subtraction), * (multiplication), / (division), % (modulo/remainder)
Integer division vs. float division: 7 / 2 = 3 (integer truncation) vs. 7.0f / 2.0f = 3.5f (float division)
String concatenation: combining strings with + or string interpolation ($"Player health: {health}")
Console I/O: Console.WriteLine() for output, Console.ReadLine() for input, and type conversion (int.Parse, float.Parse) for converting string input to numbers
Naming conventions: camelCase for variables (playerHealth), PascalCase for classes and methods (CalculateDamage), descriptive names that explain the variable's purpose
Constants: 'const' keyword for values that never change (const float Gravity = 9.81f); prevents accidental modification of critical values
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a text-based number-guessing game that declares variables for the secret number, the player's guess, and the attempt count, reads input from the console, and provides feedback.
setup_requirements: Visual Studio Code with C# extension installed
.NET SDK installed (version 8.0 or later)
Terminal/command line basics: navigating directories, running 'dotnet new console', 'dotnet run'

review.value: A named location in memory storing a value of a specific type. The name describes its purpose; the type determines valid operations.
int for whole numbers, float for decimals (~7 digits), double for precise decimals (~15 digits), string for text, bool for true/false.
The compiler rejects operations on incompatible types, catching bugs at compile time rather than at runtime. This is a feature, not an inconvenience.
Using $"text {variable}" to embed variable values in strings. Cleaner and less error-prone than concatenation with + operators.

learning_objectives: Write if, else if, and else statements to branch program execution based on conditions
Use comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=) and logical operators (&&, ||, !)
Implement nested conditionals and switch statements for multi-branch decision logic
Build a text-based game scenario with meaningful branching choices
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 3
Head First C# by Andrew Stellman, Ch. 2
Microsoft C# Documentation: Selection Statements (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Programs that only execute line by line from top to bottom are not interactive. Conditionals give your program the ability to make decisions, which is the foundation of every game. When a player presses the attack button, the game checks: is an enemy in range? Does the player have enough stamina? Is the weapon equipped? Each of these checks is a conditional that branches the program's execution.

A conditional evaluates a boolean expression, a statement that is either true or false. 'health > 0' is true when the player is alive and false when they are dead. 'score >= 100' is true when the player has enough points to advance. These expressions can be combined with logical operators: '&&' (AND) requires both conditions to be true, '||' (OR) requires at least one to be true, and '!' (NOT) inverts the result.

Nested conditionals allow complex decision trees. A text adventure might check: is the player in the dungeon? If yes, do they have a torch? If yes, they can see the treasure. If no, they stumble in the dark. Each level of nesting adds a layer of condition-dependent behavior. However, deeply nested conditionals become hard to read and maintain, so we will also learn techniques for flattening them.
key_concepts: Boolean expressions: statements that evaluate to true or false; the foundation of all conditional logic
if statement: executes a code block only when a condition is true; the simplest form of branching
else and else-if: providing alternative execution paths for when conditions are false or when multiple conditions need checking
Comparison operators: == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less or equal), >= (greater or equal)
Logical operators: && (AND: both true), || (OR: at least one true), ! (NOT: inverts truth value)
Short-circuit evaluation: && stops evaluating if the first condition is false; || stops if the first is true; useful for avoiding null reference errors
Nested conditionals: if statements inside other if statements; powerful but become hard to read beyond 2-3 levels of depth
Switch statement: multi-branch selection for discrete values (matching menu choices, character classes, game states); cleaner than long if/else-if chains
Ternary operator: 'condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse' for simple inline conditional assignment
Guard clauses: checking for invalid conditions early and returning/continuing before the main logic; reduces nesting and improves readability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a text adventure room with multiple branching paths: the player enters a room with a door, a window, and a chest. Each choice leads to different outcomes based on conditional logic. Adding inventory checks (has key? has torch?) using boolean variables.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous session
Starter code file with room description template (provided on course page)

review.value: A control structure that executes different code blocks depending on whether a boolean expression evaluates to true or false.
&& requires both conditions true, || requires at least one true, ! inverts. Used to build complex decision logic from simple boolean checks.
Check for invalid conditions early and exit/return before the main logic. Reduces nesting depth and makes the code's happy path easier to follow.
Cleaner than long if/else-if chains when branching on discrete values. Each case handles one value; break prevents fall-through.

learning_objectives: Write for, while, and do-while loops for repeated execution with correct termination conditions
Use loops to process collections of game data (enemy waves, inventory items, score tables)
Identify and fix common loop errors: off-by-one errors, infinite loops, and incorrect termination conditions
Apply nested loops for grid-based operations (game boards, tile maps)
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 4
Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Introduction (online)
Microsoft C# Documentation: Iteration Statements (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Games are built on repetition. Every frame is a loop iteration. Every enemy in a wave is processed in a loop. Every item in an inventory is checked in a loop. Every tile in a game board is drawn in a nested loop. Mastering loops is essential because they are the construct that lets your program do the same thing many times without writing the same code many times.

C# provides three loop structures, each suited to different situations. The for loop is best when you know in advance how many iterations you need: 'spawn 10 enemies,' 'draw 8 inventory slots,' 'check 64 chess squares.' The while loop is best when the number of iterations depends on a runtime condition: 'keep fighting until someone dies,' 'keep asking for input until the player types a valid command.' The do-while loop guarantees at least one execution before checking the condition: 'show the menu at least once before checking if the player wants to quit.'

The most common bug in programming is the off-by-one error: a loop that runs one too many or one too few times. This usually happens because the programmer confused < with <= or started counting from 1 instead of 0. The second most common bug is the infinite loop: a loop whose termination condition is never met because the loop body does not modify the variable being checked. Both bugs are easy to prevent with careful attention to loop bounds and termination logic.
key_concepts: for loop: 'for (int i = 0; i < count; i++)' when iteration count is known; initializer, condition, and increment are declared together
while loop: 'while (condition)' when iteration count depends on runtime state; check happens before each iteration
do-while loop: 'do { ... } while (condition)' guarantees at least one execution; check happens after each iteration
Loop control: 'break' exits the loop immediately; 'continue' skips to the next iteration; use sparingly for clarity
Off-by-one errors: the most common loop bug; using < when you mean <= or starting at 0 when you mean 1 (or vice versa)
Infinite loops: loop body fails to modify the termination condition; the program hangs forever. Always verify the condition can become false.
Nested loops: a loop inside another loop; essential for 2D structures (game boards, tile maps). The inner loop runs completely for each iteration of the outer loop.
Loop patterns for games: spawning N enemies, iterating through inventory items, counting occurrences, finding min/max values, accumulating totals
foreach loop: 'foreach (var item in collection)' iterates through every element without managing an index; cleaner for read-only traversal
Loop performance: avoid unnecessary work inside loops; move invariant calculations outside the loop body; be aware of O(n) vs O(n^2) when nesting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Spawning a wave of 10 enemies in a console game simulation using a for loop, then using a while loop to run combat rounds until all enemies or the player are defeated, tracking and displaying results.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
Enemy data file (JSON array of enemy stats, provided)
Starter code with array declaration (provided)

review.value: Best when iteration count is known at loop start. Initializer, condition, and increment declared together, making the loop's bounds explicit.
The most common loop bug. Occurs when the loop runs one too many or too few times. Always verify: does the loop start at 0 or 1? Does it use < or <=?
The inner loop runs completely for each outer iteration. For a 10x10 grid, the inner loop runs 100 times total. Essential for 2D game data (boards, maps, grids).
Cleaner syntax for iterating through all elements in a collection when you do not need the index. Preferred for read-only traversal.

learning_objectives: Declare and call functions with parameters and return values
Apply the Single Responsibility Principle to function design
Explain variable scope (local, class-level, parameter) and lifetime
Refactor a long procedural program into well-named, focused functions
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 5
Clean Code by Robert C. Martin, Ch. 3 (Functions)
Microsoft C# Documentation: Methods (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
As programs grow beyond a few dozen lines, putting everything in one block of code becomes unmaintainable. You cannot find anything, you cannot test individual pieces, and a change in one place can break something far away. Functions solve this by letting you name a reusable block of logic, making your code readable, testable, and organized.

A function (also called a method in object-oriented languages) takes input through parameters, performs a specific task, and optionally returns output. The key design principle is Single Responsibility: each function should do one thing and do it well. If you cannot describe what a function does without using the word 'and,' it probably does too much. 'CalculateDamage' is a good function name. 'CalculateDamageAndUpdateHealthAndPlaySound' is a bad one, because it has three responsibilities that should be three separate functions.

Scope determines where a variable is visible and usable. A variable declared inside a function exists only within that function and is destroyed when the function returns. A variable declared at the class level persists as long as the object exists. A parameter is a variable that receives its value from the caller. Understanding scope prevents bugs where you accidentally modify a variable that belongs to a different context.
key_concepts: Function declaration: access modifier, return type, name, parameter list, and body; 'public int CalculateDamage(int baseDamage, float multiplier)'
Parameters and arguments: parameters are the variables in the function signature; arguments are the actual values passed when calling the function
Return values: the function sends a result back to the caller using the 'return' keyword; void functions perform actions without returning
Single Responsibility Principle: each function should have one reason to change; if the description uses 'and,' split it into two functions
Local scope: variables declared inside a function are only visible within that function and are destroyed when the function exits
Class-level scope: variables declared at the class level (fields) persist as long as the object exists and are accessible from all methods in the class
Parameter passing: by value (a copy of the value, original unchanged) vs. by reference (ref/out keywords, original can be modified)
Method overloading: defining multiple functions with the same name but different parameter lists; the compiler selects the correct version based on argument types
Refactoring: restructuring existing code into functions without changing its behavior; improves readability, testability, and maintainability
Naming conventions for functions: verb-first names that describe the action (CalculateDamage, SpawnEnemy, IsAlive, GetHealth)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactoring a 100-line combat calculator into named functions: CalculateDamage(), ApplyArmor(), CheckCritical(), DisplayResult(). Then demonstrating how the refactored code is easier to test and modify.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
Unrefactored combat calculator starter code (provided)

review.value: A named, reusable block of code that takes input (parameters), performs a specific task, and optionally returns output (return value).
Each function should have one purpose. If you cannot describe it without 'and,' split it. Small, focused functions are easier to read, test, and reuse.
Local variables exist only within their declaring block. Class-level fields persist with the object. Scope controls visibility and prevents accidental cross-contamination.
Restructuring code without changing behavior. The goal is improved readability and maintainability. Refactoring is not rewriting; it is reorganizing.

learning_objectives: Declare, initialize, and access elements of arrays and List<T> collections
Use LINQ-style operations (Find, Where, Count, Any) for querying game data collections
Implement common collection patterns: searching, filtering, sorting, and aggregating
Choose the appropriate collection type (array, List, Dictionary) for different game data scenarios
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 6 (Collections)
Head First C# by Andrew Stellman, Ch. 8
Microsoft C# Documentation: Arrays and Collections (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Games are full of collections. An inventory is a collection of items. A leaderboard is a collection of scores. A tile map is a 2D collection of tile types. An enemy wave is a collection of enemy instances. Until now, you have worked with individual variables. Today you learn to work with groups of related data.

An array is the simplest collection: a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type. You declare an array with a size, and that size never changes. 'int[] scores = new int[10];' creates space for exactly 10 integers. Arrays are fast and memory-efficient, but their fixed size makes them inflexible for game scenarios where the number of elements changes at runtime.

List<T> is the go-to collection for most game programming scenarios. It is a dynamically-sized array that grows and shrinks as elements are added and removed. 'List<Enemy> enemies = new List<Enemy>();' starts empty and grows as enemies spawn. List provides methods for adding, removing, finding, and sorting elements, making it far more practical than raw arrays for gameplay logic.
key_concepts: Array declaration and initialization: 'int[] scores = new int[5];' or 'int[] scores = { 100, 85, 92, 78, 95 };'
Array access: zero-indexed (scores[0] is the first element, scores[4] is the fifth); IndexOutOfRangeException if you go past the bounds
Arrays are fixed-size: once created, the length cannot change; use when the count is known and constant
List<T>: dynamically-sized collection; Add(), Remove(), Count, Contains(), Clear(); grows automatically as elements are added
List vs. array: List for dynamic counts (enemies, inventory items); array for fixed counts (grid tiles, predefined stats)
Iterating collections: for loop with index, foreach loop without index, LINQ methods for functional-style queries
Common operations: Find (first matching element), FindAll/Where (all matching elements), Count (how many match), Any (does at least one match), Sum/Average (aggregate)
Sorting: List.Sort() with Comparison<T> delegates for custom sort orders (sort enemies by health, sort inventory by value)
2D arrays: 'int[,] grid = new int[10, 10];' for game boards and tile maps; accessed with two indices grid[row, col]
Collection initialization: using collection initializers for clean test data setup: 'var items = new List<string> { "Sword", "Shield", "Potion" };'
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an inventory system that stores items in a List, allows adding and removing items, searches for items by name, sorts by value, and displays the inventory contents with formatting.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
Item data file (JSON array of item names, types, and values, provided)

review.value: Fixed-size, zero-indexed sequence of elements of the same type. Fast and efficient but inflexible. Use when the size is known and constant.
Dynamically-sized collection that grows and shrinks at runtime. The default choice for game collections (enemies, items, projectiles).
The first element is at index 0, not 1. An array of length 5 has valid indices 0-4. Accessing index 5 causes IndexOutOfRangeException.
Functional-style methods (Find, Where, Count, Any, Sum) for querying collections without writing manual loops. Cleaner and less error-prone.

learning_objectives: Use Dictionary<TKey, TValue> for key-value data storage and O(1) lookup by key
Model game data relationships using dictionaries (item catalogs, stat tables, configuration)
Load and parse simple JSON data into dictionaries and typed objects
Choose between List and Dictionary based on access pattern requirements
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 6 (Dictionaries)
Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 2 (Command pattern, brief intro)
Microsoft C# Documentation: Dictionary<TKey,TValue> (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Many game data lookups are not sequential searches through a list but direct retrievals by a unique identifier. When the player equips item 'iron_sword,' the game needs to look up that item's stats instantly, not scan through every item in the database. When the system needs to load level 'dungeon_3,' it needs to find that level's data directly. Dictionaries provide this capability through key-value mapping with constant-time lookup.

A Dictionary<TKey, TValue> stores pairs of keys and values where each key is unique. The key is the identifier you use to look up data, and the value is the data you get back. 'Dictionary<string, int> itemPrices' lets you store and retrieve prices by item name: 'itemPrices["iron_sword"] = 150;' stores the price, and 'int price = itemPrices["iron_sword"];' retrieves it. The lookup happens in O(1) constant time regardless of how many items are in the dictionary, compared to O(n) linear time for searching a list.

Dictionaries are essential for loading game data from external files. Game data (items, enemies, levels, dialogue) is typically stored in JSON files and loaded into dictionaries or typed objects at runtime. This separation of data from code means designers can modify game content without touching the codebase, which is crucial for production workflows.
key_concepts: Dictionary<TKey, TValue>: a collection of key-value pairs where each key is unique; O(1) lookup, insertion, and deletion by key
Adding and accessing: dict[key] = value to add/update; dict[key] to retrieve; dict.ContainsKey(key) to check existence before access
KeyNotFoundException: thrown when accessing a key that does not exist; always check ContainsKey or use TryGetValue for safe access
TryGetValue: 'if (dict.TryGetValue(key, out var value))' safely checks and retrieves in one operation; the preferred pattern
Common game uses: item catalogs (string ID to item data), stat tables (character class to base stats), localization (key to translated string), configuration (setting name to value)
JSON parsing: using System.Text.Json to load JSON files into C# objects; JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(jsonString) converts JSON to typed data
Separation of data and code: storing game configuration in JSON files so designers can modify values without recompiling
Dictionary vs. List: use Dictionary when you need fast lookup by a unique key; use List when you need ordered sequences, frequent iteration, or index-based access
Iterating dictionaries: foreach (var kvp in dict) gives KeyValuePair<TKey, TValue>; dict.Keys and dict.Values for iterating only keys or values
Nested data structures: Dictionary<string, List<string>> for one-to-many relationships (character class to list of available abilities)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a game item catalog: loading item data from a JSON file into a Dictionary<string, Item>, looking up items by ID, displaying item details, and modifying item stats at runtime.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
Sample items.json data file (provided)
System.Text.Json NuGet package (included in .NET 8)

review.value: Key-value collection with O(1) lookup by unique key. The go-to data structure when you need fast retrieval by identifier.
Safe lookup pattern: checks if the key exists and retrieves the value in one operation, avoiding KeyNotFoundException.
Storing game data in JSON files and loading at runtime separates content from code. Designers can modify game balance without touching the codebase.
Dictionary for fast lookup by unique key. List for ordered sequences and iteration. Choose based on your primary access pattern.

learning_objectives: Define a class with fields, properties, constructors, and methods
Instantiate objects and call their methods to drive game behavior
Model game entities (Player, Enemy, Item, Weapon) as classes with encapsulated state
Apply encapsulation to prevent invalid object states
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 6-7
Head First C# by Andrew Stellman, Ch. 5
Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 1 (Architecture)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Object-oriented programming lets you model game entities as self-contained units that combine data and behavior. Every character, item, projectile, and system in a modern game is an object. A Player object has health data and a TakeDamage method. An Enemy object has position data and a MoveToward method. An Item object has a name and a Use method. Objects interact with each other through method calls, creating the runtime behavior of the game.

A class is the blueprint that defines what data an object holds (fields/properties) and what it can do (methods). An object is a specific instance created from that blueprint. The class 'Player' defines that every player has health, attack, defense, and a name. The object 'player1' is a specific player with 100 health, 15 attack, 10 defense, and the name 'Aria.' You can create as many objects from one class as you need: every enemy in a wave is an instance of the Enemy class, each with their own health and position.

Encapsulation is the practice of hiding an object's internal data behind controlled access points (properties and methods). Instead of letting any code in the program set player.health to -50 (an invalid state), you make health private and provide a TakeDamage(int amount) method that ensures health never drops below zero. This prevents entire categories of bugs.
key_concepts: Class: a blueprint defining the data (fields) and behavior (methods) of an object; declared with 'class Player { ... }'
Object: a specific instance of a class, created with 'new': 'Player player1 = new Player("Aria", 100);'
Fields: private variables that store the object's state (private int health; private string name;)
Properties: controlled access to fields (public int Health { get; private set; }); can include validation logic in the setter
Constructor: a special method that initializes an object when created; ensures objects start in a valid state
Methods: functions that belong to a class and operate on its data; 'public void TakeDamage(int amount)' modifies the object's health
Encapsulation: hiding internal data (private fields) and exposing controlled access (public properties/methods); prevents invalid states
The 'this' keyword: refers to the current object instance; resolves ambiguity when parameter names match field names
ToString() override: customizing how an object is displayed as a string; useful for debugging and console output
Object interaction: objects call each other's methods; player.Attack(enemy) calls enemy.TakeDamage(player.AttackPower)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building Player and Enemy classes with encapsulated health, attack/defense stats, and combat methods, then running a turn-based combat sequence between two object instances.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
UML class diagram reference sheet (provided)

review.value: A class is the blueprint (Player); an object is a specific instance (player1 with 100 HP and name 'Aria'). Many objects can be created from one class.
Making fields private and exposing them through properties/methods with validation. Prevents external code from putting objects in invalid states like negative health.
Initializes an object when created. Ensures every object starts in a valid state. If a Player always needs a name and health, the constructor requires both.
Objects communicate by calling each other's methods. This models real game interactions: player attacks enemy, enemy takes damage, item heals player.

learning_objectives: Create class hierarchies using inheritance to share common behavior
Override base class methods in derived classes to provide specialized behavior
Use polymorphism to write code that works with any type in a class hierarchy
Apply the Liskov Substitution Principle to validate inheritance relationships
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 8
Head First C# by Andrew Stellman, Ch. 6
Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 3 (Subclass Sandbox)

lecture_notes.introduction:
In a game, many entities share common characteristics. Both players and enemies have health, position, and the ability to take damage. Both swords and staves are weapons with a name, damage value, and an attack method. Rather than duplicating this shared code in every class, inheritance lets you define common behavior in a base class and have derived classes inherit and extend it.

A base class 'GameEntity' might define health, position, TakeDamage(), and IsAlive(). A derived class 'Player' inherits all of that and adds inventory, experience points, and LevelUp(). Another derived class 'Enemy' inherits the same base and adds patrol behavior and DropLoot(). The shared code exists in one place, so fixing a bug in TakeDamage() fixes it for every entity type.

Polymorphism, meaning 'many forms,' is the ability to treat objects of different derived types through a common base type reference. If you have a List<GameEntity> containing both players and enemies, you can call TakeDamage() on every element without knowing or caring which specific type it is. Each object responds according to its own overridden version of the method. This is the foundation of systems that process many different game objects uniformly.
key_concepts: Inheritance: a derived class (child) inherits all fields and methods from a base class (parent); 'class Enemy : GameEntity'
The 'base' keyword: calling the base class constructor or methods from the derived class; 'base(name, health)' in the derived constructor
Method overriding: marking a base method 'virtual' and overriding it in derived classes with 'override' to provide specialized behavior
Polymorphism: treating derived objects through a base type reference; the correct overridden method is called based on the actual object type at runtime
Abstract classes: classes that cannot be instantiated directly and may contain abstract methods that derived classes must implement; 'abstract class GameEntity'
Abstract methods: method declarations without implementation in abstract classes; derived classes must provide the implementation
The Liskov Substitution Principle: any code that works with a base type should work correctly with any derived type; if it does not, the inheritance hierarchy is wrong
Protected access modifier: visible to the class itself and all derived classes but not to external code; the middle ground between private and public
Inheritance hierarchy design: prefer shallow hierarchies (1-2 levels) over deep ones; deep hierarchies become rigid and hard to modify
When to use inheritance vs. composition: inheritance for 'is-a' relationships (an Enemy IS a GameEntity); composition for 'has-a' relationships (a Player HAS an Inventory)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a GameEntity base class, deriving Player and Enemy from it, overriding the TakeDamage() method in each (Player's version triggers a screen flash, Enemy's version drops loot on death), and processing a mixed List<GameEntity> polymorphically.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
Class hierarchy diagram template (provided)

review.value: Derived classes inherit all fields and methods from a base class. Write shared code once in the base; specialize in derived classes. 'class Enemy : GameEntity'.
Treating different object types through a common base reference. The correct overridden method is called based on the actual type, enabling uniform processing of diverse objects.
Cannot be instantiated directly. May contain abstract methods that derived classes must implement. Use when the base class is a concept, not a concrete thing.
Derived types must be substitutable for their base type without breaking correctness. If substituting causes bugs, the inheritance relationship is wrong.

learning_objectives: Implement a fixed-timestep game loop with input, update, and render phases
Explain why delta time is essential for frame-rate-independent movement
Calculate frame timing and understand the relationship between FPS, frame time, and simulation speed
Structure game code around the game loop pattern with clean phase separation
readings: Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 9 (Game Loop)
Fix Your Timestep! by Glenn Fiedler (online article, gaffer.org)
MonoGame Framework Documentation: Game Class and Update/Draw (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
The game loop is the heartbeat of every interactive application. Unlike a console program that runs once and exits, a game runs continuously, processing input, updating game state, and drawing to the screen many times per second. This continuous cycle, while(running) { processInput(); update(dt); render(); }, is the game loop, and it is the fundamental architecture pattern of all real-time games.

The most critical concept in the game loop is delta time (dt): the elapsed time between the current frame and the previous frame. Without delta time, movement speed depends on frame rate. If your game runs at 60 FPS and you move the player 5 pixels per frame, the player moves 300 pixels per second. But if the frame rate drops to 30 FPS, the player moves only 150 pixels per second, suddenly half as fast. Delta time solves this by expressing movement as 'pixels per second' multiplied by the actual time elapsed: 'position += speed * dt'. This makes movement consistent regardless of frame rate.

A fixed-timestep game loop takes this further by decoupling the simulation rate from the render rate. The simulation always advances in fixed increments (e.g., 1/60th of a second), accumulating real elapsed time and running as many fixed steps as needed to catch up. This prevents physics instability that can occur with variable timesteps while still rendering at whatever frame rate the hardware supports.
key_concepts: The game loop: while(running) { processInput(); update(deltaTime); render(); } - the fundamental architecture of all real-time games
Delta time (dt): the elapsed time in seconds since the last frame; used to make movement frame-rate independent: position += speed * dt
Frame-rate independence: without delta time, game speed is tied to frame rate; with delta time, the game runs at the same speed regardless of FPS
Fixed timestep: the simulation always advances in constant increments (e.g., 1/60s), accumulating real time and running multiple steps if needed to catch up
Variable timestep: the simulation advances by whatever dt actually occurred; simpler but can cause physics instability with large dt values
Time accumulator: stores the difference between real elapsed time and simulation time; when the accumulator exceeds the fixed step size, run a simulation step
processInput() phase: reading keyboard, mouse, and gamepad state; should not modify game state directly, only record input for the update phase
update(dt) phase: advancing game simulation by dt seconds; moving objects, checking collisions, applying physics, updating AI
render() phase: drawing the current game state to the screen; should not modify game state, only read it
FPS and frame time: 60 FPS = 16.67ms per frame; 30 FPS = 33.33ms per frame; frame time = 1000 / FPS
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a minimal game loop in C# with MonoGame: a rectangle that moves across the screen at a consistent speed regardless of frame rate, using delta time for movement. Demonstrating what happens when delta time is removed (speed tied to FPS).
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
MonoGame or Raylib-cs NuGet package installed
Basic understanding of program structure (Main method, class structure)

review.value: The continuous cycle of processInput(), update(dt), render() that runs every frame. The fundamental architecture pattern of all real-time games.
Elapsed seconds since the last frame. Multiply all movement by dt to ensure consistent speed regardless of frame rate. position += speed * dt.
Input reads input. Update modifies state. Render draws state. Mixing these phases causes bugs that are extremely hard to diagnose.
Simulation advances in constant increments regardless of real time. Prevents physics instability while allowing variable render rates.

learning_objectives: Implement keyboard and mouse input handling using both polling and event-driven models
Distinguish between 'key held,' 'key just pressed,' and 'key just released' states
Design an input abstraction layer that maps physical keys to game actions
Handle multiple simultaneous key presses for smooth, responsive player control
readings: Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 15 (Event Queue)
MonoGame Documentation: Keyboard and Mouse Input (online)
Input Best Practices for Games (Game Developer article)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Input handling is how the player communicates with the game. A responsive, well-designed input system makes the game feel crisp and immediate. A sluggish or inconsistent one makes even great gameplay feel broken. Getting input right is one of the most important technical challenges in game development.

There are two fundamental approaches to input. Polling checks the current state of all input devices every frame: 'Is the W key currently held down? Is the left mouse button pressed?' This is simple and works well for continuous actions like movement. Event-driven input responds to discrete state changes: 'The W key was just pressed this frame. The left mouse button was just released.' This works better for one-shot actions like jumping, shooting, or opening a menu.

Most games need both. Movement uses polling (check if the key is held every frame for smooth movement). Jumping uses events (trigger once when pressed, not continuously while held). The input system must also handle multiple simultaneous keys: pressing W and D together should produce diagonal movement, not override one direction with the other.
key_concepts: Polling: checking the current state of input devices every frame; KeyboardState.IsKeyDown(Keys.W) returns true as long as W is held
Event-driven: detecting state transitions (pressed this frame, released this frame); requires comparing current and previous frame states
Key states: 'held' (currently down), 'just pressed' (down this frame, up last frame), 'just released' (up this frame, down last frame)
Previous state tracking: storing the last frame's input state and comparing with the current frame to detect transitions
Input abstraction layer: mapping physical keys to named game actions (Keys.W maps to 'MoveUp', Keys.Space maps to 'Jump') so controls can be remapped
Simultaneous input: reading multiple keys per frame; movement input should combine (W+D = diagonal) not override
Input buffering: storing recent inputs for a short window to forgive slight timing errors (pressing jump 2 frames before landing should still work)
Mouse input: position (X, Y coordinates), button state (left, right, middle), scroll wheel delta
Dead zones: for analog input (gamepad sticks), ignoring small values near center to prevent drift; typically 10-20% of the axis range
Input timing in the game loop: always read input at the beginning of the frame, before update and render, to ensure consistent behavior
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a player movement system with WASD controls using polling for smooth movement, spacebar for jumping using event detection (just pressed), and mouse click for attacking. Demonstrating diagonal movement and input buffering for the jump.
setup_requirements: Development environment with MonoGame or Raylib-cs from previous session
Basic game loop from previous session's project
Keyboard and mouse for input testing

review.value: Polling checks current state every frame (good for continuous actions like movement). Events detect state transitions (good for one-shot actions like jumping).
Map physical keys to named actions so controls can be remapped without changing game logic. 'MoveUp' instead of 'Keys.W' throughout the code.
Read all keys independently each frame. Multiple keys pressed simultaneously should combine their effects (W+D = diagonal), not cancel each other.
Store recent inputs for a short window (2-5 frames) to forgive slight timing errors. Makes the game feel responsive rather than punishing.

learning_objectives: Implement axis-aligned bounding box (AABB) collision detection between rectangular objects
Implement circle-to-circle collision detection using distance comparison
Design collision response behavior (bounce, stop, destroy, collect) for different game scenarios
Organize collision checks efficiently to avoid O(n^2) performance with many objects
readings: Game Physics Engine Development by Ian Millington, Ch. 1-2
Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 10 (Spatial Partition, brief intro)
2D Collision Detection Tutorial (Real-Time Collision Detection by Ericson, Ch. 4 excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Collision detection is how the game knows when objects interact: when a player touches a coin, when a bullet hits an enemy, when the player character walks into a wall. Without collision detection, game objects would pass through each other like ghosts. It is one of the most fundamental systems in any game.

The simplest and most common collision check is the axis-aligned bounding box (AABB) test. Every object is wrapped in a rectangle aligned with the screen axes. Two rectangles overlap if and only if their projections overlap on both the X and Y axes. This is fast (four comparisons), easy to implement, and sufficient for many 2D game scenarios.

Circle-to-circle collision is even simpler mathematically: two circles collide if the distance between their centers is less than the sum of their radii. This requires a distance calculation (or its squared version to avoid the expensive square root), but handles round objects more accurately than boxes.

Collision detection is only half the problem. Collision response, what happens when two objects overlap, is equally important and varies by game design. A player touching a coin should collect it (destroy the coin, increment score). A player touching a wall should stop (prevent movement past the wall). A ball hitting a paddle should bounce (reverse velocity). Each response requires different code, but all start with the same detection phase.
key_concepts: AABB collision: two axis-aligned rectangles overlap iff A.left < B.right AND A.right > B.left AND A.top < B.bottom AND A.bottom > B.top
Bounding box: a rectangle that fully encloses an object; may be larger than the object for simple shapes, providing approximate but fast detection
Circle collision: two circles collide if distance(centerA, centerB) < radiusA + radiusB; use squared distance to avoid square root for performance
Collision response types: stop (walls), collect (pickups), bounce (physics), damage (enemies), trigger (zones, checkpoints)
Collision pairs: when checking N objects against each other, the naive approach checks N*(N-1)/2 pairs; optimization is needed for large object counts
Spatial partitioning: dividing the game world into regions so objects only check for collisions with others in the same region; quadtrees, grids, spatial hashing
Collision layers: grouping objects by type and only checking relevant combinations (bullets collide with enemies, not with other bullets)
Tunneling: fast-moving objects can pass through thin obstacles between frames; solved by swept collision checks or limiting maximum speed
Trigger zones vs. physical colliders: triggers detect overlap without blocking movement (entering a room, crossing a checkpoint); physical colliders block movement
Encapsulating collision logic: putting detection in a reusable method (bool CheckCollision(Rectangle a, Rectangle b)) that the game loop calls for every relevant pair
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a simple collection game: a player rectangle moves with keyboard input, coin rectangles are scattered on screen, and AABB collision detection triggers collection (coin disappears, score increments). Adding wall rectangles that stop player movement.
setup_requirements: Development environment with game loop and input handling from previous sessions
Basic rendering setup for drawing colored rectangles
Multiple game objects defined with position and size

review.value: Two rectangles overlap iff: A.left < B.right AND A.right > B.left AND A.top < B.bottom AND A.bottom > B.top. Four comparisons, very fast.
Two circles collide if the distance between centers is less than the sum of radii. Use squared distance (dx*dx + dy*dy < (r1+r2)*(r1+r2)) to avoid expensive square root.
Detection asks 'are these objects overlapping?' Response asks 'what happens now?' Same detection logic, different response code for walls, pickups, enemies, and triggers.
Group objects by type and only check relevant pairs. Bullets check against enemies, not against each other. Reduces unnecessary checks dramatically.

learning_objectives: Implement basic 2D physics: velocity, acceleration, and gravity using Euler integration
Apply friction and drag forces to create natural-feeling movement
Design platformer physics with gravity, jumping, and ground detection
Tune physics parameters (gravity strength, jump force, friction coefficient) for desired game feel
readings: Game Physics Engine Development by Ian Millington, Ch. 3-4
Nature of Code by Daniel Shiffman, Ch. 1-2 (Vectors, Forces)
The Game Feel Guide (Steve Swink's Game Feel, Ch. 2-3)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Physics gives game objects the illusion of weight, momentum, and physical presence. Without physics, objects move like cursor arrows: instant start, instant stop, no sense of mass. With physics, a character accelerates when you push the joystick, decelerates when you release it, and is pulled downward by gravity. This transforms mechanical inputs into movements that feel alive.

The fundamental physics equation for games is Euler integration: velocity += acceleration * dt; position += velocity * dt. Acceleration is the force applied to the object (gravity, player input, friction). Velocity is the object's current speed and direction. Position is where the object is on screen. Each frame, acceleration changes velocity, and velocity changes position. This simple chain produces surprisingly realistic movement when the parameters are tuned correctly.

Gravity is the most common force in 2D games. A constant downward acceleration of roughly 980 pixels/second-squared (scaled for your game's coordinate system) pulls every object toward the ground. Jumping applies an instantaneous upward velocity that fights against gravity, producing a parabolic arc. The interplay between jump force and gravity strength determines how jumpy, floaty, or heavy your character feels, and tuning these values is one of the most important aspects of game feel.
key_concepts: Velocity: speed and direction of movement; a 2D vector (vx, vy) measured in units per second
Acceleration: rate of change of velocity; gravity is a constant downward acceleration; player input is a horizontal acceleration
Euler integration: velocity += acceleration * dt; position += velocity * dt; the simplest integration method and sufficient for most 2D games
Gravity: constant downward acceleration (typically 500-1500 pixels/s^2 depending on game scale); applied every frame to all affected objects
Jumping: applying an instantaneous upward velocity (negative vy) that gravity decelerates, producing a parabolic arc; jump height = v0^2 / (2 * gravity)
Ground detection: checking if the object's bottom edge overlaps with a ground surface; when grounded, stop applying gravity and reset vertical velocity to 0
Friction: a force opposing horizontal movement that decelerates the object when on the ground; friction coefficient determines how quickly objects stop
Air resistance / drag: a force opposing movement proportional to velocity; prevents objects from accelerating indefinitely; drag = velocity * dragCoefficient
Coyote time: allowing the player to jump for a few frames after leaving a platform edge; compensates for human reaction time and makes jumping feel generous
Variable jump height: releasing the jump button early reduces upward velocity, giving the player control over jump height; holding the button gives maximum height
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a platformer movement system: a character with gravity, jump, ground detection, horizontal acceleration/friction, coyote time, and variable jump height. Tuning physics parameters to achieve three different game feels: heavy (like Castlevania), floaty (like Kirby), and tight (like Celeste).
setup_requirements: Development environment with game loop, input, and collision from previous sessions
Platform objects (ground rectangles) for ground detection
Physics parameters spreadsheet for tuning values (provided)

review.value: velocity += acceleration * dt; position += velocity * dt. The fundamental physics update, run every frame, that produces all movement behavior.
Gravity is constant downward acceleration. Jumping is instant upward velocity. The ratio of jump velocity to gravity determines jump height and arc shape.
Allowing jumps for a few frames after leaving a platform. A small mercy that makes platforming feel responsive rather than punishing.
Physics parameters (gravity, jump force, friction, acceleration) determine how the character feels. Tuning is iterative and subjective but makes enormous difference.

learning_objectives: Implement a finite state machine using an enum and switch statement to manage game object behavior
Design state transitions with clear entry and exit conditions
Apply state machines to common game problems: character states, AI behavior, menu navigation
Identify when a state machine is the right pattern versus when other approaches are better
readings: Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 7 (State)
AI for Games by Ian Millington, Ch. 5 (State Machines)
Finite State Machines in Game Development (Gamasutra tutorial)

lecture_notes.introduction:
A state machine is one of the most useful patterns in game programming. It models an object that can be in exactly one state at a time and transitions between states based on events or conditions. A player character might be in the Idle, Walking, Jumping, Attacking, or Hurt state. An enemy might be in Patrol, Chase, Attack, or Retreat. A menu system might be in MainMenu, Settings, Gameplay, or Paused.

Without a state machine, managing complex behavior leads to tangled conditional logic: 'if the player is jumping and they press attack and they are not already attacking and they are not hurt...' This spaghetti code is hard to write, harder to debug, and almost impossible to extend. A state machine replaces it with a clean structure: each state knows what it does and what transitions lead to other states. Adding a new state means adding a new case, not modifying every existing condition.

The simplest implementation uses an enum for the possible states and a switch statement in the update method. Each case handles the behavior for that state and checks for transition conditions. When a transition occurs, the machine sets the new state and optionally runs exit logic for the old state and entry logic for the new state.
key_concepts: Finite state machine (FSM): an object is in exactly one state at a time; events or conditions trigger transitions to other states
State: a distinct mode of behavior with its own update logic, allowed actions, and transition rules
Transition: a change from one state to another, triggered by a condition (input, timer, collision, health threshold)
Entry and exit actions: code that runs once when entering or leaving a state (play animation, reset timer, enable/disable collisions)
Enum-based implementation: 'enum PlayerState { Idle, Walking, Jumping, Attacking, Hurt }' with switch in Update()
State transition diagram: a visual representation showing states as circles and transitions as labeled arrows; essential for planning complex behavior
Guard conditions: requirements that must be met for a transition to occur (can only attack if not in Hurt state, can only jump if grounded)
Common game FSM applications: player character states, enemy AI behavior, game flow (menu, playing, paused, game over), animation state management
Hierarchical state machines: states that contain sub-states (Combat state contains Light Attack, Heavy Attack, Block sub-states); for complex behavior
When not to use a state machine: when states are few and simple, a state machine adds unnecessary complexity; when states need to overlap, consider a different pattern
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a player character state machine with Idle, Walking, Jumping, and Attacking states, each with distinct behavior, transitions, and animations. Drawing the state transition diagram on the whiteboard, then implementing it in code.
setup_requirements: Development environment with game loop, input, collision, and physics from previous sessions
Character with basic animation support
Whiteboard or paper for state transition diagrams

review.value: An object in exactly one state at a time, with defined transitions between states. Replaces tangled conditional logic with clean, extensible structure.
Visual representation with states as circles and transitions as labeled arrows. Draw this before writing code. If you cannot diagram it, you cannot implement it.
Code that runs once on state change: entering Attacking plays the attack animation; exiting Attacking resets the attack timer. Keeps state-specific setup clean.
Requirements for transitions: can only jump if grounded, can only attack if not stunned. Guards prevent invalid state transitions and maintain game rules.

learning_objectives: Read and write game data to files using JSON serialization
Design a save game system that captures and restores game state
Handle file I/O errors gracefully (missing files, corrupted data, permission issues)
Apply the principle of separation of concerns to save/load architecture
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 10
Microsoft Documentation: System.Text.Json (online)
Save Game Design Patterns (Game Developer article)

lecture_notes.introduction:
A game that cannot save progress forces the player to start over every time they close the application. Save systems are expected in virtually every genre and are one of the first features players notice when it is missing or broken. Today we learn how to capture game state, write it to a file, and restore it later.

The core concept is serialization: converting in-memory objects (player stats, inventory contents, world state) into a format that can be written to a file and later deserialized back into objects. JSON is the most common format for game save data because it is human-readable (useful for debugging), widely supported (libraries exist in every language), and flexible enough to handle complex nested data structures.

The save system architecture should be separated from the game logic. The game objects should not know or care how they are saved. Instead, a SaveManager collects the relevant data from game objects, packages it into a serializable structure, writes it to a file, and reverses the process on load. This separation means the save format can change without modifying game logic, and game logic can change without breaking save files.
key_concepts: Serialization: converting in-memory objects to a storable format (JSON string); deserialization: converting the stored format back to objects
JSON format: human-readable text format with key-value pairs, arrays, and nested objects; the standard for game save data
System.Text.Json: C# built-in library for JSON serialization; JsonSerializer.Serialize() and JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>()
Save data structure: a dedicated class (SaveData) containing all data that needs persisting; separate from game runtime objects
File operations: File.WriteAllText() writes a string to a file; File.ReadAllText() reads a file into a string; File.Exists() checks if a file exists
Error handling for I/O: try-catch around file operations; handle FileNotFoundException (first launch, no save yet), JsonException (corrupted save), UnauthorizedAccessException (permission denied)
Save file location: using Environment.GetFolderPath() for platform-appropriate save directories; never hardcode paths
Auto-save design: saving at checkpoints, on significant events, or at regular intervals; balancing save frequency against I/O performance
Save data versioning: including a version number in save files so the game can handle format changes between updates without crashing
Separation of concerns: game objects expose their saveable state through properties; the SaveManager handles serialization and file I/O; neither knows the other's implementation details
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete save/load system for the RPG project: creating a SaveData class, collecting player stats, inventory, and progress into it, serializing to JSON, writing to file, and loading on game start. Demonstrating error handling for missing and corrupted save files.
setup_requirements: Development environment from previous sessions
RPG project with player stats and inventory
File system access for reading and writing save files

review.value: Converting objects to JSON for file storage. Deserialization converts JSON back to objects. The bridge between runtime memory and persistent storage.
A dedicated SaveData class contains only persistable data. Game objects expose their state; SaveManager handles I/O. Neither knows the other's implementation.
Always wrap file operations in try-catch. Handle missing files (first launch), corrupted data (bad JSON), and permissions (restricted directories) gracefully.
Include a version number in save files. When the format changes between game updates, the version number lets you migrate old saves or fail gracefully.

learning_objectives: Present a complete, playable 2D game demonstrating all course concepts
Explain technical architecture decisions with clarity and confidence
Receive peer feedback on code quality, game feel, and design choices
Reflect on programming growth across the semester and identify areas for continued learning
readings: Review all course materials and your code from the semester
Prepare presentation materials for final game demonstration

lecture_notes.introduction:
This final session is your opportunity to present the 2D games you have been building throughout the semester. Each of you will demonstrate your game, walk the class through your technical architecture, and explain the programming decisions you made. This is a celebration of how far you have come from 'what is a variable?' to 'here is a complete, playable game.'

Your presentation should cover three areas. First, the game itself: show it working, let us see the gameplay, and explain the core mechanic. Second, the architecture: how is the code organized? What classes did you create? How does the game loop drive everything? How are collisions handled? Third, the challenges: what was the hardest bug you encountered? How did you solve it? What would you do differently if you started over?

After presentations, we will conduct a course retrospective. We have covered variables, conditionals, loops, functions, arrays, lists, dictionaries, classes, inheritance, polymorphism, game loops, input handling, collision detection, 2D physics, state machines, and file I/O. That is a complete foundation for game programming. The language and patterns you have learned transfer directly to any game engine (Unity, Godot, Unreal) because the concepts are universal even when the syntax differs.
key_concepts: Game demonstration: showing the game running, highlighting key features, and letting the audience understand the gameplay through observation
Architecture walkthrough: explaining class hierarchy, game loop structure, state management, and data flow through the system
Code quality discussion: naming conventions, encapsulation, single responsibility, separation of concerns as evidenced in the project code
Bug story: describing the most challenging bug, the debugging process, the root cause, and the fix; demonstrates problem-solving skills
Peer feedback: constructive observations about code organization, game feel, visual polish, and potential improvements
Course synthesis: connecting 15 weeks of concepts into a unified understanding of game programming
Transfer to engines: how variables, classes, game loops, collision, physics, and state machines map directly to Unity/Godot/Unreal concepts
Next steps: advanced programming courses, game engine tutorials, game jams, portfolio development, and open-source contribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student game presentations (8-10 minutes each) with live gameplay demonstration, technical walkthrough, and peer Q&A.
setup_requirements: Completed final game project, built and runnable
Presentation slides covering architecture and key technical decisions
Source code accessible for code quality discussion

review.value: A game with a loop, input, collision, physics, state management, and save/load. Built from scratch, understanding every line. This is the foundation for everything that comes next.
Clean code with proper OOP, single responsibility functions, encapsulated state, and separated concerns is easier to debug, extend, and maintain than clever but tangled code.
Every programmer encounters hard bugs. The value is in the debugging process: isolating the problem, forming hypotheses, testing them, and understanding the root cause.
Variables, classes, loops, game loops, collision, physics, state machines, and serialization work the same way in every game engine. You now understand the concepts behind the tools.

quizzes.topic: Variables, Data Types, Conditionals, and Loops

questions.question: Write a C# for loop that iterates through an array of integers called 'enemyHealthValues' and prints the index and value of every enemy whose health is above 50. Include the array declaration with at least 5 sample values.

rubric.key: Correct array declaration and initialization with 5+ values
Correct for loop syntax with proper bounds (.Length, not hardcoded)
Correct conditional checking health > 50 inside the loop
Output includes both index and value with correct formatting

questions.answer: The loop condition checks 'score < 100' but the loop body never modifies the variable 'score.' Since score remains 0 forever, the condition is always true and the loop never terminates. Fix: add a statement inside the loop body that increases score, for example 'score += 10;' after the WriteLine. This ensures score eventually reaches or exceeds 100, making the condition false and terminating the loop.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies that score is never modified inside the loop body
Explains why an unmodified condition variable causes infinite execution
Provides a correct fix that modifies score inside the loop to eventually terminate it

questions.question: Write a C# switch statement that takes a string variable 'direction' (values: "north", "south", "east", "west") and prints the appropriate room description for each. Include a default case for invalid input.

rubric.key: Correct switch syntax with string variable and four cases
Each case has a break statement preventing fall-through
Default case handles invalid input with informative message

questions.question: Write a C# function called 'FindStrongestEnemy' that takes a List<int> of enemy health values and returns the index of the enemy with the highest health. If the list is empty, return -1.

rubric.key: Correct function signature with List<int> parameter and int return type
Handles empty list edge case by returning -1
Correctly finds the index of the maximum value using a tracking variable
Loop starts at index 1 and compares correctly using the current max index

questions.question: Explain the difference between an array and a List<T> in C#. Give a game development scenario where each is the better choice and explain why.
questions.answer: An array has a fixed size set at creation that cannot change; it is fast and memory-efficient but inflexible. A List<T> has a dynamic size that grows and shrinks as elements are added or removed; it provides methods for adding, removing, and searching but uses slightly more memory. Array is better for a fixed game board: a chess board is always 8x8, so int[8,8] is the right choice because the size never changes and array access is the fastest possible. List is better for an enemy wave manager: enemies spawn and die during gameplay, so the count changes continuously; List<Enemy> allows Add() when spawning and Remove() when killed without manual array resizing.

rubric.key: Correct key difference: fixed size (array) vs. dynamic size (List)
Array scenario is appropriate with reasoning about why fixed size is beneficial
List scenario is appropriate with reasoning about why dynamic size is needed

questions.question: Write a C# code snippet that uses a Dictionary<string, int> to store item prices, adds three items, then safely looks up the price of an item that may or may not exist using TryGetValue.

rubric.key: Correct Dictionary declaration with string keys and int values
Three items added with distinct keys and reasonable values
Uses TryGetValue (not direct access) for safe lookup
Handles both found and not-found cases with appropriate messages

questions.question: Write a C# class called 'Weapon' with: a private string field 'name', a private int field 'baseDamage', a private float field 'critMultiplier', a constructor that takes all three values, and a method 'CalculateDamage(bool isCritical)' that returns baseDamage if not critical, or (int)(baseDamage * critMultiplier) if critical.

rubric.key: Correct class declaration with three private fields of correct types
Constructor properly initializes all three fields from parameters using 'this'
CalculateDamage has correct signature, return type, and conditional logic

questions.question: Explain what polymorphism is in object-oriented programming. Then describe a game scenario where you have a List<GameEntity> containing both Player and Enemy objects, and explain how polymorphism lets you process them uniformly.
questions.answer: Polymorphism means 'many forms': the ability to treat objects of different derived types through a common base type reference, with each object responding according to its own overridden implementation. Scenario: A game has a List<GameEntity> containing both Player and Enemy objects. Each frame, the game loop iterates through the list and calls entity.Update(deltaTime) on every element. The Player's overridden Update() checks for keyboard input and moves accordingly. The Enemy's overridden Update() runs AI patrol logic. The game loop does not need to know which specific type each entity is; it just calls Update() and polymorphism ensures the correct behavior executes. This means adding a new entity type (NPC, Projectile) only requires creating a new class that inherits from GameEntity and overrides Update(), with zero changes to the game loop.

rubric.key: Correct definition of polymorphism as treating derived types through a base reference
Game scenario demonstrates uniform processing of different types through a shared interface
Explains the extensibility benefit: new types require no changes to existing processing code

questions.question: Explain encapsulation and describe a specific game development scenario where failing to encapsulate data leads to a bug.
questions.answer: Encapsulation is hiding an object's internal data (private fields) and exposing access only through controlled methods or properties. This prevents external code from putting the object into an invalid state. Bug scenario: A Player class has a public 'health' field. Enemy AI code sets player.health = player.health - damage without bounds checking. When damage exceeds current health, health goes negative (-15), and the UI displays a negative health bar. The game also fails to trigger death because the death check only looks for health == 0, not health < 0. If health were encapsulated behind a TakeDamage(int amount) method, that method would clamp health to minimum 0 and trigger death when health reaches 0, preventing both the negative display and the missed death trigger.

rubric.key: Correct definition of encapsulation as hiding data behind controlled access
Specific scenario with a concrete bug caused by exposed internal data
Explains how encapsulation would have prevented the bug

quizzes.topic: Game Loop, Input, Collision Detection, and Physics

questions.question: Explain what delta time is and why it is essential for game programming. Then show, with a code example, what goes wrong when movement is not multiplied by delta time.
questions.answer: Delta time (dt) is the elapsed time in seconds since the last frame. It makes movement frame-rate independent. Without dt: 'position.X += 5;' moves 5 pixels per frame. At 60 FPS, the object moves 300 pixels/second. At 30 FPS, it moves 150 pixels/second, half as fast. The game's speed is tied to the frame rate, so the game runs in slow motion on weak hardware and fast-forward on powerful hardware. With dt: 'position.X += 300 * dt;' moves 300 pixels per second regardless of frame rate. At 60 FPS, dt is ~0.0167, so each frame moves ~5 pixels (300 * 0.0167). At 30 FPS, dt is ~0.0333, so each frame moves ~10 pixels (300 * 0.0333). The per-frame amount differs, but the per-second speed is identical.

rubric.key: Correct definition of delta time as elapsed seconds since last frame
Clearly explains the problem without delta time (speed tied to FPS) with concrete numbers
Shows how multiplying by dt produces consistent speed with concrete math

questions.question: Write the AABB collision detection condition as a C# boolean expression, given two Rectangle structs A and B, each with properties Left, Right, Top, Bottom. Then explain what happens if you use > instead of < in one of the comparisons.

rubric.key: All four AABB conditions correct with proper operators
Explains the effect of inverting one comparison on collision behavior
Identifies that the inversion would cause false positives and false negatives

questions.question: Explain the three phases of the game loop (processInput, update, render) and why it is important to keep them separate. Give an example of a bug that could occur if update and render logic were mixed together.
questions.answer: processInput reads the current state of input devices (keyboard, mouse, gamepad) and records it for use by the update phase. update advances the game simulation: moves objects, applies physics, checks collisions, runs AI, and updates all game state using delta time. render draws the current game state to the screen without modifying it. Keeping them separate ensures predictable behavior: input is always read before state changes, state is always fully updated before drawing, and drawing never accidentally changes game state. Bug example: if collision checking (update logic) is mixed into the render phase, and the render phase draws enemies in order, then an enemy drawn early in the frame has its collision checked before later enemies are drawn. If collision destroys the enemy and removes it from the list while iterating, this causes a collection-modified-during-iteration exception or skips enemies. With proper separation, all updates complete before any rendering begins, preventing this class of bug entirely.

rubric.key: All three phases correctly described with their responsibilities
Explains why separation matters for predictable behavior
Provides a specific, plausible bug caused by mixing phases

quizzes.topic: Physics, State Machines, and File I/O

questions.question: Write the two lines of Euler integration code that update velocity and position each frame, given variables: Vector2 velocity, Vector2 acceleration, Vector2 position, and float deltaTime. Then explain what happens if you swap the order of the two lines.

rubric.key: Both Euler integration lines correct with proper operators and deltaTime multiplication
Correctly explains that swapping causes position to use stale velocity
Describes the observable gameplay effect of the swap (sluggish feel, delayed response)

questions.question: Describe what a finite state machine is and explain why it is better than nested if/else chains for managing a game character's behavior states (idle, walking, jumping, attacking).
questions.answer: A finite state machine (FSM) models an object that is in exactly one state at a time, with defined transitions between states triggered by conditions. For character behavior: the character is in Idle, Walking, Jumping, or Attacking state. Each state has its own update logic and transition rules. FSM is better than nested if/else because: (1) Readability: each state's logic is isolated in its own case/class, not tangled with other states. 'What does the character do while jumping?' is answered in one place. (2) Extensibility: adding a new state (Swimming) means adding one new case with its transitions, not modifying every existing condition in the chain. (3) Correctness: the FSM guarantees the character is in exactly one state, preventing impossible combinations (jumping while attacking while idle) that nested conditions can accidentally allow. (4) Debuggability: printing the current state name instantly tells you what the character should be doing, versus tracing through nested conditions to figure out which branch executed.

rubric.key: Correct definition of FSM as one-state-at-a-time with defined transitions
At least two distinct advantages over if/else chains with clear reasoning
Demonstrates understanding of why mutual exclusivity prevents bugs

questions.question: Write a C# code snippet that saves a player's name (string) and score (int) to a JSON file using System.Text.Json, with error handling for the file write operation.

rubric.key: SaveData class with correct property types for serialization
Correct use of JsonSerializer.Serialize and File.WriteAllText
Error handling with try-catch that catches specific, relevant exceptions

major_assignments.title: Console RPG Battle System
major_assignments.description: Build a text-based RPG battle system in C# that demonstrates mastery of all programming fundamentals covered in weeks 1-8. The system must support a player fighting through a sequence of enemies with turn-based combat, an inventory of usable items, a weapon/equipment system, and a simple leveling mechanism. All game data must be loaded from JSON files.
objectives: Apply OOP principles to model game entities with proper encapsulation and inheritance
Implement a game loop that drives turn-based combat with clear state management
Use collections (List, Dictionary) and JSON loading for data-driven game content
Handle all edge cases and invalid input gracefully without crashes
deliverables: Complete C# project with source code, JSON data files, and README explaining how to build and run
At least 5 classes demonstrating inheritance: GameEntity base, Player, Enemy (with at least 2 derived enemy types), Item, Weapon
Combat loop supporting: Attack, Use Item, Defend, and Flee actions
At least 4 distinct enemies with different stats and at least 2 with unique behaviors (e.g., healing, area attack)
Items and enemies loaded from JSON files, not hardcoded
Input validation handling non-numeric input, out-of-range choices, empty inventory, and all edge cases

criteria: Code compiles and runs without errors on first attempt
Class hierarchy uses inheritance correctly with shared code in base classes
Encapsulation is consistent (private fields, public properties/methods, no invalid states possible)
Functions follow Single Responsibility Principle with clean naming
Turn-based combat loop functions correctly with clear player feedback at each step
Damage calculation accounts for attack, defense, weapon stats, and critical hits
Items have meaningful, varied effects (healing, buffs, damage, status effects)
Leveling system adjusts player stats after defeating enemies with visible feedback
All input validated gracefully without crashes (non-numeric, out-of-range, empty inventory, edge cases)
Game data loaded from JSON files with error handling for missing or malformed files
Game ends properly with distinct win and lose conditions and appropriate messages
No infinite loops, unreachable code paths, or states where the game cannot progress
Enemies have behaviors beyond different stat values (healing, multi-attack, status effects)
Combat feedback is clear, informative, and formatted for easy reading
At least one feature beyond minimum requirements (status effects, equipment system, shop, boss fights)
README is clear with build instructions and the game is genuinely playable and enjoyable
Well-structured OOP code with proper inheritance, encapsulation, and separation of concerns
Fully functional turn-based combat with meaningful player choices and clear feedback
Robust data loading and error handling for all edge cases
Creative, polished game experience that goes beyond minimum requirements

major_assignments.title: 2D Game Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a 2D game using a game loop architecture with real-time keyboard input, AABB collision detection, basic 2D physics (gravity, jumping), and a state machine for character behavior. The game must have a clear objective, scoring system, and win/lose conditions, all running at a consistent frame rate with frame-rate-independent movement.
objectives: Implement a fixed-timestep game loop with separated input, update, and render phases
Use delta time for frame-rate-independent movement and physics
Detect and respond to AABB collisions for multiple interaction types (collect, block, damage)
Manage character behavior using a finite state machine
deliverables: Complete C# project using MonoGame, Raylib-cs, or equivalent 2D framework with build instructions
Player character controlled by keyboard with physics-based movement (acceleration, friction, gravity, jumping)
At least 8 collectible objects and at least 3 obstacle/enemy objects with different collision responses
Character state machine with at least 4 states (Idle, Moving, Jumping, plus one additional)
On-screen HUD displaying score, health/lives, and current state
Save/load system that persists high score between sessions using JSON file I/O

criteria: Game loop has clear separation of input, update, and render phases
Delta time is used correctly for all movement and physics calculations
State machine manages character behavior with clean transitions and no invalid states
