Save/load system correctly persists and restores data with error handling
AABB collision detection is correctly implemented for all object interactions
Different collision types (collect, block, damage) trigger appropriate responses
No false positives or missed collisions at normal gameplay speeds
Physics feel natural with tuned gravity, jump force, and friction values
Keyboard input is responsive with no perceptible lag
Multiple simultaneous key presses handled correctly for smooth diagonal movement
Game runs at stable frame rate without stuttering or frame drops
Code is organized into classes with proper encapsulation and separation of concerns
Clear win/lose conditions communicated to the player through UI and feedback
HUD is accurate, updated in real time, and does not obstruct gameplay
Game has a beginning, gameplay with meaningful choices, and an ending
Additional creative features beyond minimum requirements (sound, particles, difficulty progression, power-ups)
Solid game loop architecture with proper delta time and phase separation
Correct collision detection and physics with well-tuned game feel
Clean state machine managing character behavior
Polished, playable game with clear objectives, feedback, and save system

major_assignments.title: Final Game: Complete 2D Game with All Course Concepts
major_assignments.description: Expand your 2D game prototype into a complete, polished game that integrates every concept from the course. The final game must include OOP architecture, a game loop with physics, collision detection with multiple response types, a state machine for character and game flow management, JSON-based data loading for game content, and a save system. Present the game to the class with a technical walkthrough.
objectives: Synthesize all programming concepts into a cohesive, functional game
Demonstrate code quality through proper OOP, clean architecture, and robust error handling
Produce a game that is genuinely fun to play, not just technically correct
Present technical decisions clearly and defend architectural choices
deliverables: Complete, polished C# game project with all source code, data files, and comprehensive README
At least 3 levels or equivalent content progression with increasing difficulty
Game content (enemy stats, level layouts, item properties) loaded from JSON data files
Full character state machine with at least 6 states including game-specific states
Game flow state machine (MainMenu, Playing, Paused, GameOver, Victory)
Save system with high scores and optionally level progress
10-minute class presentation with live gameplay, architecture walkthrough, and bug story

criteria: OOP architecture with proper inheritance hierarchy, encapsulation, and polymorphism
Game loop with correct delta time, phase separation, and stable frame rate
Collision detection handles all game interactions correctly
State machines manage both character behavior and game flow cleanly
Physics feel polished with well-tuned parameters for the game's genre
All game content loaded from JSON with graceful error handling
Save/load system works correctly across sessions
Code is clean, well-organized, and follows naming conventions throughout
Game has multiple levels or content stages with clear progression
Difficulty increases meaningfully across levels
Game is genuinely fun and engaging, not just technically functional
Additional creative features that enhance the experience beyond minimum requirements
README provides clear build instructions and game description
Presentation covers gameplay, architecture, and key technical decisions within time limit
Bug story demonstrates debugging skills and problem-solving process
Code is commented where non-obvious decisions are made (not excessive commenting)
Clean, well-architected codebase demonstrating all course concepts
Polished, playable game with content progression and save system
Technical presentation that demonstrates understanding of architecture decisions
Creative, engaging game experience that goes beyond technical requirements

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) assessing programming concepts from variables through state machines and file I/O, combining code-writing and conceptual questions

lab_assignments.description: Weekly coding exercises completed during lab sessions, graded on correctness, code quality, and proper application of the week's concepts

major_assignments.description: Console RPG Battle System (150 pts), 2D Game Prototype (150 pts), and Final Complete 2D Game (200 pts)

midterm_examination.description: Take-home coding exam covering weeks 1-8: variables, control flow, functions, collections, OOP, inheritance, and polymorphism with both implementation and analysis questions

final_project.description: Final 2D game integrating all course concepts with in-class presentation, technical walkthrough, and code quality review

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is required for all lab sessions where hands-on exercises are completed. Lecture attendance is strongly recommended. More than 3 unexcused lab absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Code that does not compile receives a maximum of 50%. Extensions require a request at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussing concepts and debugging strategies with classmates is encouraged. All submitted code must be written by you. Copying code from classmates, the internet, or AI tools without understanding and attribution constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own work. You may reference documentation, textbooks, and official tutorials. Any external code snippets used must be cited in comments. Using AI code generators to complete entire assignments is prohibited; using them to understand a concept is permitted with disclosure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a computer with .NET SDK 8.0+, Visual Studio Code with C# extension, and a MonoGame or Raylib-cs installation for 2D game development. Lab computers are available but personal laptops are recommended. A keyboard is required for game input testing.
